item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A short skip? What a delight :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted because this could be so much more!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really don't care for the "we can't talk about it except in generalities because that's the way it is" trope, and the beauty and hauntingness that others experienced left me cold. On the other hand, the last paragraph was good enough to lead me to a novote.

item.title: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded:
All this talk about meaning, but I'm just infatuated with the way you seem to take SCP inspiration from songs (at least in this and Fly By Night Only).

Enjoy!
https://youtu.be/4z0EjPU4A7M

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, someone did an explainer for this article on /r/SCPdeclassified, so I'll just clarify one thing: The list title, "Doomsday", is a reference to this song. It doesn't really convey any information about the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well then the Foundation should hire me, because I would love to document this thing! And of anything goes wrong, I can always use that time traveling scip to stop myself from trying such a thing. On second thought… :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… While the concept is interesting, it still doesn't make up for the astonishing and so far rather implausibly-explained deficiency of information on this anomalous entity/object. We have more on the actively self-censoring -055 than we do on this thing. I think that's a clear sign that something is wrong with this skip.

Perhaps this is a side effect of the word limit, but I don't think you're really keying onto the whole "personnel are reluctant to even go near this thing" bit. It's a bit of a cop-out, yes, but in the "practical sense", people who find a task sufficiently unpleasant will avoid doing it even if they are naturally curious about it. Why would they be reluctant? Good question, but maybe it's 2808 objecting to the whole "people doing work that bureaucracy wants them to do". Effectively coercing people into resisting bureaucracy might muddle 2808's message, but I see no reason that it needs to be an elegant, self-consistent artwork in the same way that like, half of the things the Foundation contains don't work as their creator intended.

  1. I'm beginning to develop a distinct hatred for word-limit skip-writing contests, since this is the 2nd one I've come across, and both it and the previous one seem to have been done more harm than good by this word limit.
  2. The "personnel are reluctant to even go near this thing" bit does not just feel like a cop-out, but a lazy cop-out at that. Such a phenomenon should've raised alarm bells in the Foundation's ranks that a memetic compulsion is running uncontained (they're no strangers to highly dangerous memes, cognitohazards, and infohazards), and in concurrence with what Kalinin said, I find it highly implausible that none of the Foundation's scientists would have the motivation and resolve to research the damn thing out of their own volition, if only to confirm whether or it poses a threat to humanity.
  3. Nobody seems to have a counter for the case of "some people work under a bureaucratic mandate because they want to do so of their own accord".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An early attempt to neutralize SCP-2808's anomalous properties by strategic alterations to the Foundation's charter resulted in SCP-2808 inadvertently becoming the Director of Site-47. Attempts to remove SCP-2808 have been unsuccessful.

Old-school, Series I-style humor but with a more restrained tone. One sentence, then you move on, then you're out. I dig it. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: fucking finally we get some others

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(though I'm all in favour of shooting down any form of bureaucracy…!)

Same here, comrade.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oof.
Yeah, it helps a bit. But I definitely seem to have great problems with the abstract, and this is too abstract for me.
(though I'm all in favour of shooting down any form of bureaucracy…!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The art piece (and that it changes) makes it in particular.

I find it pretty funny that I keep getting compliments on the fact that it changes, when that was literally done the night before as an afterthought. But it's the one thing about the piece that people consistently like enough to mention. Heh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought this was beautiful and clever, as well as thematically solid and appropriate for the contest in a similar way to lurkd's (also controversial) entry. The art piece (and that it changes) makes it in particular.

As an aside…

There is, and never has been, any rule that all information on an SCP that the Foundation has must be included in an SCP article.

Every single SCP on the site omits reams of information — real scientific/government documentation massively outstrips our page counts — but we've developed a tradition of pointless text walls to achieve a purely illusory verisimilitude, at the expense of most or all readability (and, often, a decrease in artistic quality).

This SCP does have an excuse to be described with brevity, but it still could have been a wall of text, and written by someone else outside the context, likely would have been. Therefore, this piece succeeds as a work for the contest as well as (in my opinion) artistically, even ignoring the excuse it has for brevity.

(this comment brought to you by the department of hypocritical walls of text)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Stock commentary on the bleakness and futility of bureaucracy. With the added caveat that bureaucracy and art are diametrically opposed. These sentiments are consistent with popular stereotypes, but not so much with reality. Even artists use sidewalks and call customer service when their cell phone stops working. And people who work in large organizations didn't all undergo a soul-ectomy as a condition of employment. As a result, the central conceit lacks resonance.

I could really go on about how your rationale here is flawed (it kinda sucks actually), but it's not terribly relevant to the article, because I don't entirely agree with what you think is the central conceit either.

This article, in its original form, was an exercise in Weber — the idea being that bureaucracies function because they are run by professionals who are interchangeable, and the anomaly would synonymize a person with their bureaucratic position. When I was looking for an idea for this contest, I decided to scrap that idea for parts — the Foundation is fundamentally a bureaucracy, so I decided to mess with that aspect of it. My point is that the thing described in this article is definitely commentary, but this article is not intended to function as social commentary and it doesn't pose a theoretical situation in which any particular belief is shown to be correct, and I don't think it's fair to judge it on that metric.

What I think is the central conceit, beyond showing off my art, is still as a thought exercise in an anomaly that is inimical to proper containment by virtue of how the Foundation goes about containment. It's closer to my SCP-868 in that regard than anything — 868-as-an-article doesn't posit that amnesticization is bad, only that there is opposition; similarly, 2808-as-an-article does not posit that bureaucracy is anti-art (or vice versa), only that there is opposition.

In a more practical sense, I don't buy that there's so little information on this. The Foundation is made up of, in most interpretations of it, a fuckload of scientists. Scientists are likely to be at least a little more curious than your baseline person, and many will work on stuff in their spare time simply because it's neat to learn new things. I would bet that at least a few people would see this in the course of their normal work on other stuff, go "wow, that's really interesting," and gather data on it out of a sense of their own personal fulfillment. And that's really what this piece is missing; sometimes, people do work under the aegis of an Official Bureaucracy(tm) because that's what they want to do.

Perhaps this is a side effect of the word limit, but I don't think you're really keying onto the whole "personnel are reluctant to even go near this thing" bit. It's a bit of a cop-out, yes, but in the "practical sense", people who find a task sufficiently unpleasant will avoid doing it even if they are naturally curious about it. Why would they be reluctant? Good question, but maybe it's 2808 objecting to the whole "people doing work that bureaucracy wants them to do". Effectively coercing people into resisting bureaucracy might muddle 2808's message, but I see no reason that it needs to be an elegant, self-consistent artwork in the same way that like, half of the things the Foundation contains don't work as their creator intended.

The world isn't strictly a Kafka novel.

Um, citation needed? Have you seen the world and a Kafka novel together before? No? I didn't think so. Wake up, America.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Stock commentary on the bleakness and futility of bureaucracy. With the added caveat that bureaucracy and art are diametrically opposed. These sentiments are consistent with popular stereotypes, but not so much with reality. Even artists use sidewalks and call customer service when their cell phone stops working. And people who work in large organizations didn't all undergo a soul-ectomy as a condition of employment. As a result, the central conceit lacks resonance.

In a more practical sense, I don't buy that there's so little information on this. The Foundation is made up of, in most interpretations of it, a fuckload of scientists. Scientists are likely to be at least a little more curious than your baseline person, and many will work on stuff in their spare time simply because it's neat to learn new things. I would bet that at least a few people would see this in the course of their normal work on other stuff, go "wow, that's really interesting," and gather data on it out of a sense of their own personal fulfillment. And that's really what this piece is missing; sometimes, people do work under the aegis of an Official Bureaucracy(tm) because that's what they want to do. The world isn't strictly a Kafka novel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, that's a little passive-aggressive, if you ask me. I really am tired of "we can't give a description of it, for the sake of having a format screw".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I simply don't get it. I didn't get it upon reading (several times), and I don't get it much more after reading the comments.

If you try to do anything at all about 2808 because it's your job, your job becomes something else ('beautifier') and nobody can do your old job because they get overwhelmed by hallucinations. If you do anything about 2808 not because it's your job but because you think it's in line with your job, it automatically becomes your job, then your job becomes something else and nobody can do your old job.

Does this help at all?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article contains all, or at least most, of the information that people have felt like gathering, compiling, and publishing purely of their own accord. Alternatively, some of it might have required sacrificing a few unimportant personnel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm rather confused as to how the information contained in the article manages to avoid the thing's effects. Overall, though, this is one of the more effective articles that use their anomalous nature to cover for their word limit. The stuff that's missing ultimately doesn't really matter, the effect is interesting, and it generally comes together rather well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not sure about this - not sure about anything concerning this, except I like your pictures very much, Scantron! - but it seems I'm not the target audience.

I simply don't get it. I didn't get it upon reading (several times), and I don't get it much more after reading the comments.

Especially this:

Actions taken regarding SCP-2808 informally, but under bureaucratic pressure or direct influence, are spontaneously added to that individual's official responsibilities. They then become impossible to carry out due to the aforementioned hallucinations.

Um… WHAT????? (this is the sentence I read even more times than the whole skip! It just doesn't sink in…)

Well-written and probably clever, but not clear enough to me. Downvoted. Sorry!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've gotten a few compliments on the art, so I'm gonna direct people who are interested to my Tumblr, where I post a lot more like them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What I'm saying is that 2808 is meant to trip up people who think like you, and if you can't handle the ambiguity of it, then you're not the target audience.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
are you calling me an ASS backwards? D:
oh fuck the image changes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Do you miSS humAnity, TL333s?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is like a more concrete 055, which is why I'm downvoting. Well, that and I'm just incensed that there's no way to give even a basic description of what this is. (Is it material? Alive? A concept? Give me something, please.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I first read through, I started freaking out because how could this not be Keter? It's a skip as an unimpeachable Site Director for crying out loud?

Then I read it again and realized it's never taken action on its own, and figured out that this thing probably isn't sapient.

Then you said you wanted to demonstrate how it messes with the Foundation bureaucracy, and it clicked.

You get a +1, and I've got a headcanon where the director of Site-47 is a soft pretzel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dig it. I see as an embodiment of primal disorder which prevents the ordered functions which human society works on from functioning. That's the meaning of "Doomsday". Or maybe not. Whatever, I like it.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted, because I find it yet another entry that tries making the scip in question difficult to write about in order to fit within the word count.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The effect is not the most interesting to me, but the art was beautiful, and I enjoyed the overall feeling. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No vote.

FWIW, I've already written two thematically similar pieces: SCP-920-EX (the workgroup printer) and SCP-2553 (the juridicial person).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I do think the way it's written now, where it's not tied to anything in particular, is the best way to present it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is definitely the kind of thing certain elements of AWCY? would get up to, but I had neither reason nor space to give them a shout-out. Personally, though, I like to imagine that this is something that predates bureaucracy and has developed a distaste for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked all the images (nice job on making those) and the SCP itself was rather interesting. I'm getting some AWCY vibes from it; this seems to be the kind of stunt they'd pull. The Fallacy of Bureaucracy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It was strange. And surprisingly fun.

I did guess who it had written it (having the memory of a goldfish) by the second paragraph. Take that as you will.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I know that 2808 is incomprehensible even in-universe, I find it hard to attach myself to an SCP if it is defined (even as a concept).

I suppose that's a risk I take, but I decided that withholding the information would be infinitely more interesting than any definite thing I put in there. I also wanted to demonstrate, rather than simply state, that 2808 is committed to posing an impediment to smooth bureaucratic functioning; I figured that refusing to pin it down to any conceivable form would do the trick.

EDIT: The pictures! By God! Okay, brilliant use. Slightly cheap, but I will accept it.

"While fascinating, this phenomenon seemed to have no relevance to SCP-190 SCP-2808's true purpose, other than as a mere parlor trick, to entertain babes and fools."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pros:

  • The procedures is evocative and acts as a good hook to the rest of the work.
  • EDIT: The pictures! By God! Okay, brilliant use. Slightly cheap, but I will accept it.

Cons:

  • While I know that 2808 is incomprehensible even in-universe, I find it hard to attach myself to an SCP if it is ill-defined (even as a concept).

Verdict: Neutral vote Upvote (curse me for submitting to veil pictures)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is probably one of my favorite entries. It's beautiful in its own way, and I love what SCP-2808 (the anomaly and the article) is trying to accomplish and say.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's my entry for the Short Works contest, clocking in at 234 words. All images (yes, plural — refresh the page) are original works by myself.

I'll leave the meaning of the title ("Doomsday") as an exercise for the reader.

The original concept of this was just for a regular SCP, and it would have gone well over 500 words; it seemed to fit the idea of this contest, though, so I repurposed it.
